core_intuitions: The granularity tradeoff: words explode the vocabulary and hit OOV, characters/bytes explode sequence length (and O(n^2) attention), so subwords are the Goldilocks middle — frequent strings whole, rare strings split, nothing OOV.
BPE is a greedy loop: repeatedly merge the most frequent adjacent pair into a new symbol, yielding an ORDERED merge list; encoding re-applies those merges in learned (rank) order, not left-to-right.

Byte-level BPE starts from the 256 byte values, a complete and finite base alphabet, so any string in any language is representable and there is provably no <unk> — worst case a novel glyph falls back to its individual UTF-8 bytes.

Three algorithms, different objectives: BPE (most frequent pair) and WordPiece (max likelihood gain) build UP by merging; Unigram prunes DOWN a big vocabulary via EM and decodes by Viterbi over a segmentation lattice.
Vocabulary size is a tradeoff: larger V compresses text (fewer tokens = cheaper inference, more context) but grows embedding/softmax matrices linearly and risks undertrained glitch tokens.

Digit/letter representation determines capability: inconsistent number chunking scrambles place value and breaks arithmetic; per-digit (or 1-3-digit) splitting keeps place value positional and consistent.

existing.name: bpe-merges
existing.issue: Correctly and legibly renders in both light and dark themes; accent highlights the active merge and green marks the final tokens; reading order is clear. The one subtlety is that the figure actually depicts ENCODING (applying learned merges to the word 'lowest' via rank priority), while it is anchored just before the training-by-hand section, which could blur the train/encode distinction for a first-time reader.
existing.improvement: Optional, low priority: add a one-line subtitle such as 'applying learned merges in rank order' so it is unambiguous this is the encode-time cascade, not the training-time pair-counting loop. No structural change needed otherwise.

new_figures.name: granularity-spectrum
new_figures.concept: Why subwords: the vocabulary-size vs sequence-length tradeoff across words, subwords, and characters/bytes.
new_figures.rationale: This is the founding insight of the chapter's opening section ('Why Subwords? The Granularity Problem') and the justification for everything that follows. The prose argues it three ways (OOV, sequence length, O(n^2) attention) but there is no single visual that lets a reader SEE why the middle is the sweet spot. A static comparison makes the whole motivation click at a glance.
new_figures.brief: A three-panel comparison across a horizontal 'coarse -> fine' spectrum, with the middle panel visibly marked as the Goldilocks sweet spot. Use the SAME short example in every panel so the contrast is honest. LEFT panel 'Words': tokenize 'tokenization is running' -> three whole-word boxes [tokenization][is][running], and beside it a typo/novel word 'tokenizatoin' collapsing to a single [<unk>] box. Annotations: 'vocabulary 100k+ and unbounded', 'OOV: novel words lost', 'run / runs / running get unrelated IDs (morphology wasted)'. RIGHT panel 'Characters / bytes': tokenize 'tokenization' -> a long row of 12 single-character boxes [t][o][k][e][n][i][z][a][t][i][o][n]. Annotations: 'vocabulary ~256 (tiny)', 'no OOV ever', but 'sequences 4-5x longer -> attention cost O(n^2) blows up'. CENTER panel 'Subwords' (highlighted with the theme-safe accent, labeled 'the sweet spot'): tokenize 'tokenization is running' -> [token][ization][ is][ runn][ing] (show a couple of pieces carrying a leading-space marker). Annotations: 'fixed vocab ~30k-256k', 'frequent words whole, rare words split into reusable pieces', 'no OOV', '~1.3 tokens/word'. BELOW all three, draw two opposing gradient bars/axes that make the tradeoff geometric: 'vocabulary size increases ->' growing toward the WORDS (left) side, and 'sequence length increases ->' growing toward the CHARS (right) side, with the subword center sitting at the crossover valley where both are moderate. Small illustrative integers only; invent no benchmark numbers. Must read cleanly left-to-right and be fully understandable as one static frame in both themes.

new_figures.name: byte-level-no-oov
new_figures.concept: Byte-level BPE: every string -> UTF-8 bytes -> one of exactly 256 base symbols, so there is provably no <unk>.
new_figures.rationale: The 'no OOV, guaranteed' property is one of the chapter's key takeaways and the reason GPT-2/3/4 and Llama-3 are byte-level, but the mechanism (finite 256-value base alphabet + worst-case byte fallback + the Ġ space glyph) is spread across dense prose and code. A single flow figure makes the guarantee concrete and memorable.
new_figures.brief: A top-to-bottom pipeline that follows one deliberately tricky input all the way down, emphasizing that the base alphabet is always the finite set of 256 bytes. STAGE 1 (input): the string 'cafe 🤖' but with an accented e (cafe with acute) and a robot emoji — chosen because these characters may never have been seen at training time. STAGE 2 (UTF-8 encode): each character expands into its bytes, drawn as a row of small byte boxes with hex labels — c/a/f = one byte each, the accented e = TWO bytes, space = one byte, 🤖 = FOUR bytes (e.g. 0x63 0x61 0x66 0xC3 0xA9 0x20 0xF0 0x9F 0xA4 0x96). Make the multi-byte characters visually bracketed so the reader sees '1 char -> several bytes'. STAGE 3 (closed alphabet): a compact, clearly-labeled 16x16 grid (256 cells) titled 'every byte is one of exactly 256 values', with the bytes from stage 2 lit up inside it. Key annotation in the accent color: 'base alphabet is complete and finite -> any byte is a guaranteed token -> provably no <unk>'. STAGE 4 (merges + worst case): show frequent byte pairs merging into subword tokens (e.g. the ASCII 'caf' bytes gluing up), and show the novel emoji's worst case: it simply stays as its 4 individual byte tokens — still valid, still lossless. Include a small side callout for the bytes-to-unicode trick: 'byte 0x20 (space) -> printable glyph Ġ', explaining the Ġthe appearance. Add a short contrast chip: 'char-level BPE would emit <unk> on an unseen glyph; byte-level cannot'. Use only the small illustrative bytes shown; no invented statistics. Must be legible top-to-bottom and self-contained as a static frame in both themes.

new_figures.name: digit-place-value-scramble
new_figures.concept: Inconsistent number chunking hides place value and breaks arithmetic; per-digit / fixed-size grouping keeps it aligned.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter flags this as 'the most consequential and least obvious pitfall' and gives it an Interview Corner, yet it is explained only in prose and code. The intuition — that token boundaries failing to line up with units/tens/hundreds columns is what breaks carrying — is inherently spatial and lands far harder as a side-by-side column-alignment picture than as words.
new_figures.brief: A two-panel contrast built around a column-addition layout (the load-bearing visual is the vertical place-value grid). Use the same two illustrative numbers in both panels: 12345 and 6789, stacked for addition. LEFT panel 'Frequency-trained BPE (inconsistent)': tokenize 12345 as [123][45] and 6789 as [67][89] — deliberately different boundary positions. Draw faint vertical column guides for units / tens / hundreds / thousands / ten-thousands, and show that the token-boundary brackets do NOT align to these columns (a boundary falls mid-column). Annotation in a caution tone: 'token cuts don't match place value -> the model can't line up units-with-units -> carrying is hard to learn'. Add a small note: 'equal-length numbers even chunk differently run to run, depending on which digit strings were frequent'. RIGHT panel 'Per-digit / 1-3-digit grouping (consistent)': tokenize 12345 as [1][2][3][4][5] and 6789 as [6][7][8][9]. Now every token is exactly one column wide, so units sit under units and tens under tens — the brackets line up perfectly with the column guides. Annotation in the accent/positive tone: 'every digit is its own stable token -> place value is positional and consistent -> carrying is learnable (Llama splits digits; GPT-4 groups 1-3).' At the bottom, a one-line tie-in chip to the same disease: 'strawberry -> [str][aw][berry]: individual letters are never exposed, so the model cannot count its r's.' Use only the small integers shown; invent no benchmarks. Both panels must be legible side by side and understandable as a single static frame in both themes.

new_figures.name: unigram-segmentation-lattice
new_figures.concept: Unigram scores ALL segmentations of a string under a unigram LM and picks the Viterbi-best path — top-down probabilistic, unlike BPE's single fixed merge order.
new_figures.rationale: Unigram/SentencePiece is a full section covering the T5/Gemma/Llama family, but it is served only by prose, an EM list, code, and a comparison table. The core idea — a lattice of competing segmentations with one highlighted best path (and the ability to sample alternates for subword regularization) — is exactly the kind of concept that clicks as a picture and stays fuzzy as text. Nice-to-have rather than foundational, hence lower priority.
new_figures.brief: Draw the segmentation lattice for a short word, e.g. 'hello', as a left-to-right DAG. Nodes are the 6 character-boundary positions 0|1|2|3|4|5 laid out along a horizontal axis (between the letters h e l l o). Edges are candidate subword pieces that span from one boundary to a later one, each drawn as an arc labeled with its piece text and a small illustrative log-probability weight — include overlapping options so the branching is visible: single chars 'h','e','l','l','o'; pieces 'he','ll','llo','lo'; and the whole 'hello'. Highlight the Viterbi best path (the route maximizing the sum of log-probs) in the theme-safe accent color — for the toy weights, make 'hello' as one high-probability piece the winning path, with 'he'+'llo' as a close runner-up shown faintly. Add two contrast captions: LEFT 'BPE / WordPiece: build up by a fixed merge order -> one deterministic segmentation'; RIGHT (on the lattice) 'Unigram: score every segmentation under p(x1)...p(xk), take the best path (Viterbi) — and optionally SAMPLE an alternate path for subword regularization', with one non-best path drawn as a dashed 'sampled alternative'. Keep the lattice small (a handful of nodes and edges) so labels never overlap. Use only small illustrative probabilities; invent no real numbers. Must be fully readable as a static frame in both themes.

summary: Concept-rich but figure-poor chapter (one existing figure). The existing bpe-merges encode-cascade is a clean KEEP in both themes (optional subtitle tweak to flag it as encode-time). Proposed 3 foundational new static figures plus 1 priority-3 nice-to-have: (1) granularity-spectrum — the words/subwords/chars tradeoff that motivates the entire chapter; (2) byte-level-no-oov — the finite-256-byte base alphabet that guarantees no <unk>; (3) digit-place-value-scramble — the column-alignment picture behind the arithmetic/counting failures; (4, priority 3) unigram-segmentation-lattice — the Viterbi-best-path-over-a-lattice idea that distinguishes Unigram. All static: single frames convey each mechanism, so no animation is warranted.
